title: “Monks, such is the peril in the case of forms. Furthermore, monks, one sees that woman’s body thrown in the charnel ground, one, two, or…
content:
“Monks, such is the peril in the case of forms. Furthermore, monks, one sees that woman’s body thrown in the charnel ground, one, two, or three days dead – bloated, discolored, festering. What do you think, monks? Has the former charm and beauty disappeared and peril become evident?”
“Yes, venerable sir.”
“Monks, such is the peril in the case of forms.”

title: “Monks, why is it called form? It is deformed (ruppati), monks, therefore it is called form. Deformed by what? Deformed by cold, deformed by heat,…
content: “Monks, why is it called form? It is deformed (ruppati), monks, therefore it is called form. Deformed by what? Deformed by cold, deformed by heat, deformed by hunger, deformed by thirst, deformed by contact with flies, mosquitoes, wind, heat, and serpents. It is deformed, monks, therefore it is called form. And why, monks, is it called feeling? It feels (vedeti), monks, therefore it is called feeling. What does it feel? It feels pleasure, it feels pain, it feels neither-pain-nor-pleasure. It feels, monks, therefore it is called feeling. And why, monks, is it called perception? It perceives (sañjānāti), monks, therefore it is called perception. What does it perceive? It perceives blue, it perceives yellow, it perceives red, it perceives white. It perceives, monks, therefore it is called perception.”

title: “And what, monks, is the Dhamma leading to the full understanding of all clinging? Dependent on the eye and forms, eye-consciousness arises. The meeting of…
content: “And what, monks, is the Dhamma leading to the full understanding of all clinging? Dependent on the eye and forms, eye-consciousness arises. The meeting of the three is contact. With contact as condition, feeling. Seeing thus, a well-taught noble disciple becomes disenchanted with the eye, with forms, with eye-consciousness, with eye-contact, with feeling. Being disenchanted, he becomes dispassionate. Through dispassion, he is liberated. By being liberated, he knows: ‘I have fully understood clinging.’ Dependent on the ear and sounds … on the nose and odors … on the tongue and tastes … on the body and touches … Dependent on the mind and phenomena, mind-consciousness arises. The meeting of the three is contact. With contact as condition, feeling. Seeing thus, a well-taught noble disciple becomes disenchanted with the mind, with phenomena, with mind-consciousness, with mind-contact, with feeling. Being disenchanted, he becomes dispassionate. Through dispassion, he is liberated. By being liberated, he knows: ‘I have fully understood clinging.’ This, monks, is the Dhamma leading to the full understanding of all clinging.”

title: - What fetters the world? What leads the world? By abandoning what thing Are all fetters cut? - Delight fetters the world, Seeking leads the…
content:
- What fetters the world?
What leads the world?
By abandoning what thing
Are all fetters cut?
- Delight fetters the world,
Seeking leads the world,
By abandoning craving,
All fetters are cut.

title: “Monks, there is no form that is permanent, everlasting, eternal, not subject to change, that will remain forever. There is no feeling … perception ……
content: “Monks, there is no form that is permanent, everlasting, eternal, not subject to change, that will remain forever. There is no feeling … perception … formation … consciousness that is permanent, everlasting, eternal, not subject to change, that will remain forever.”

title: “Having seen with wisdom, the defilements are abandoned. Monks, that monk is called one who has blinded Māra, destroyed his track, escaped the sight of…
content: “Having seen with wisdom, the defilements are abandoned. Monks, that monk is called one who has blinded Māra, destroyed his track, escaped the sight of Māra. He has crossed beyond all clinging in the world. He walks confidently, stands confidently, sits confidently, lies down confidently. Why is that? Because he has escaped the reach of Māra.”

title: “Furthermore, monks, a noble disciple reflects: ‘Sensual pleasures present and future, perceptions of sensual pleasure present and future, forms present and future, perceptions of forms…
content: “Furthermore, monks, a noble disciple reflects: ‘Sensual pleasures present and future, perceptions of sensual pleasure present and future, forms present and future, perceptions of forms present and future — both are impermanent. What is impermanent is not fit to delight in, not fit to welcome, not fit to cling to.’ While he practices thus, dwells often thus, his mind becomes serene in that element. With serene mind, he attains the Imperturbable either in this very life or he becomes strongly inclined to wisdom. At the breakup of the body, after death, it is possible that his consciousness, proceeding accordingly, may attain the Imperturbable.”

title: Behold this decorated image — A heap of wounds, a collection of sores, Diseased, the object of many lustful thoughts, Nothing stable or permanent here.…
content:
Behold this decorated image —
A heap of wounds, a collection of sores,
Diseased, the object of many lustful thoughts,
Nothing stable or permanent here.

Behold this adorned appearance,
With jewels and earrings,
A skeleton covered by skin,
Made beautiful by clothes.

The feet are painted with lac and dye,
The face is smeared with powder and paste —
Enough to fool a fool,
But not one who seeks the far shore.

The hair is curled in seven rings,
The eyes are rubbed with collyrium and rouge —
Enough to fool a fool,
But not one who seeks the far shore.

title: “Monks, I will teach you about suffering, the origin of suffering, the cessation of suffering, and the path leading to the cessation of suffering. Listen.…
content: “Monks, I will teach you about suffering, the origin of suffering, the cessation of suffering, and the path leading to the cessation of suffering. Listen. And what, monks, is suffering? It should be said: the five aggregates of clinging. What five? The form aggregate of clinging … the consciousness aggregate of clinging. This, monks, is called suffering. And what, monks, is the origin of suffering? It is this craving that leads to renewed existence … craving for non-existence. This, monks, is called the origin of suffering. And what, monks, is the cessation of suffering? It is the remainderless fading away and cessation of that same craving, the abandoning and relinquishing of it, freedom from it, non-reliance. This, monks, is called the cessation of suffering. And what, monks, is the path leading to the cessation of suffering? It is the Noble Eightfold Path, namely: right view … right concentration. This, monks, is called the path leading to the cessation of suffering.”

title: Thoughts of lust Are the sensual pleasures of humans, The beautiful things in the world Are not themselves sensual pleasures. Thoughts of lust Are the…
content:
Thoughts of lust
Are the sensual pleasures of humans,
The beautiful things in the world
Are not themselves sensual pleasures.
Thoughts of lust
Are the sensual pleasures of humans.
The beautiful things remain
As they are in the world,
But the wise here
Subdue their lust.
